raw_payload.author: YoungCriticMovies
raw_payload.content:
Despite being one of the most iconic love stories ever written, Emily Brontë's Wuthering Heights has never had a definitive screen adaptation, with many filmmakers and onscreen pairings failing to capture the complex push-pull relationship at its core. This is not for a lack of trying; the novel may be one of the most adapted books in cinema history, with the likes of William Wyler, Luis Buñuel, and Andrea Arnold all taking a crack at it. Yet the adaptation that has best captured the spirit of the novel may actually be a song: Kate Bush's "Wuthering Heights." Now Emerald Fennell takes her own stab at it, pushing the limits of controversy with Wuthering Heights (2026).

Wuthering Heights once again follows the timeless love story of Catherine (Margot Robbie), the sole daughter of a gambling, spendthrift nobleman (Martin Clunes) in 19th-century England. She falls into a forbidden romance with Heathcliff (Jacob Elordi), the beggar boy her father adopts who grows up working in the estate's stables. When a wealthy bachelor (Shazad Latif) moves nearby, Catherine must choose between the promise of economic stability and surrendering to her consuming love and desire.

Wuthering Heights rests on the tried-and-true romantic premise of forbidden love, where tension arises from longing and the inability to fully express one's emotions. Fennell, ever the provocateur, seeks to undo that restraint, plunging her adaptation into full-blown erotica. She does so through an ahistorical aesthetic, blending modern sensibilities into 19th-century settings and costuming. It's an approach reminiscent of Sofia Coppola's Marie Antoinette (2006), which successfully embraced impressionism over strict historical accuracy. Fennell similarly clashes the corseted, repressed narrative with contemporary romantic tropes, particularly those rooted in the fantasy romance novels so popular today. It is a bold but misguided choice. In attempting to bring Brontë's novel down from its mythic pedestal, Fennell instead drags it into the territory of glossy fantasy romance. This is not a slight against the genre, executing heightened sexual fantasy convincingly is no easy task, but it simply doesn't harmonize with the emotional architecture of this story.

One of the main reasons that this Wuthering Heights doesn't work is the dismantling of the central forbidden desire that made it such a romantic story of longing; Fennell's Heathcliffe and Catherine consummate their love and lust for each other... frequently. While this gives the adaptation a steamy edge absent from previous versions, it robs the story of anticipation and sexual tension. Fennell's film, thus, begins to feel less like an adaptation and more like an erotic fan fiction.

Fennell first burst onto the scene with her work on Killing Eve (2018-2022) and Promising Young Woman (2020), striking a sharp balance between kitsch and satire. That equilibrium began to wobble in Saltburn (2023), though it still retained wit and dark humor. With Wuthering Heights, however, Fennell seems to have succumbed to her most indulgent instincts, losing any sense of tonal balance. Instead, the approach feels more like an intentional attempt to make a film "so bad it's good," yet the desperate self-consciousness just makes it cringey.

As with any romance, the central pair must convince us of their chemistry and inevitable longing. While Robbie and Elordi are undeniably talented and occasionally bring a knowing, tongue-in-cheek energy to their performances, they lack genuine chemistry together. Their glances feel hollow, their motivations driven more by superficial lust than by a crackling emotional connection. Though they join a lineage of accomplished actors who have portrayed these indelible characters, from Laurence Olivier to Juliette Binoche, they leave behind a distinct chill rather than smoldering passion.

Fennell retains her striking visual style, even if her symbolism can verge on the overly blunt. These flourishes at least inject some vitality into the adaptation. Yet her relentless pursuit of provocation ultimately undermines the very elements that make Wuthering Heights such a gripping love story. Despite boasting an impressive cast, the central romance lacks the electric charge the narrative demands. The modern flourishes occasionally intrigue, but they cannot rescue the film from feeling like a glossy, oversexed fan fiction. In the end, if you want a moving, faithful evocation of the novel's haunting romance, you're better off putting on Kate Bush.

raw_payload.author: peter0969
raw_payload.content:
Personally, this movie is the kind of example where sometimes, too much creative freedom can really get out of hand.

Emerald Fennell's take on the famous novel Wuthering Heights is visually stunning and appealing, but unfortunately, it's gothic and emotional connection with it's characters and structure feels lifeless, disjointed, and lacking. It's no that Fennell does offer some interesting direction choices within the movie. Especially with the gorgeous make-up, camerawork, production designs and framing is great. Many of the colorful production, sound designs, costumes and make-up really adds to the atmosphere, tone, and setting throughout. Alongside with the shot compositions, the musical score and the song writing from Charli XCX is one of the best aspects. As both the classical, yet, modern pop techno beats actually do have some nice pacing and sound composition, which I do appreciate that interesting decision.

Sadly, it's pretty clear Fennell doesn't a good job on exploring the writing and characters of Wuthering Heights, rather, Fennell doubles down on it, as if she wanted to make her take on the novel, but with a disjointed approach. Throughout, many of the writing choices, character developments, and decisions are questionable, disjointed, and uneven. As if Fennell is trying to explore different themes within the same time, yet, can't seem to focus on which one she really wants to explore. The way how Fennell structures the characters and dynamics feel very lifeless and awkward.

The characters of Catherine and Heathcliff don't feel as memorable nor impactful. Unlike the novel and previous adaptations, the main two leads have little to zero proper dynamics, which makes many of the emotional moments and charm pretty difficult to believe nor care about. Because as a whole, it feels quite butchered and messy. It's a shame because the performances are all pretty good. Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi both are really good and the other performances are doing their best to give their characters some light. But the messy writing and lacking of character engagement doesn't save the movie.

Alongside with the awkward dialogue, it's pretty clear that Fennell is talented but she is one of the good modern examples of style over substance because while she does offer some great visuals and outstanding and unique colorful takes, the writing and dynamics really need lots of work.

Overall, this adaptation is pretty disappointing. If you want to see a good adaptation, the 1939 and 2011 version are recommended.

raw_payload.author: AsianLensPlus
raw_payload.content:
There is nothing wrong with filmmakers who aim to shock through sheer audacity. But something is fundamentally broken when those efforts yield zero impact, leaving behind only a trail of grating, cringy moments. This "Wuthering Heights" feels like a hollow, fractured piece of unfinished work-parading "pretty people" through a series of banal lines and nonsensical, age-inappropriate behavior.

At times, the film fluctuates between a botox commercial and a bad school play. The music is unbelievably "on the nose," and the attempt at regional accents is grating and embarrassing. It is jarring to see such passionate, layered source material warping into nothing but an emotional void defined more by pretension than by any genuine soul. That's actually very shocking.

raw_payload.author: cdjh-81125
raw_payload.content:
Wuthering Heights might have a lot going for it, it's lavishly produced and gorgeously shot, but it's lacking in one key area: depth. Calling the writing here surface level almost feels too generous because the film almost feels like the cliff notes of a story rather than any kind of a narrative. For all the talent on display there's just nothing really here to latch on to and as someone who counts myself as an Emerald Fennell defender it's frustrating to see her give in to all of her worst tendencies as a director.

Because apart from its more explicit images and scenes it's not really clear what this film is trying to do to set itself apart as an adaption. There's a real lack of rhythm in the editing in that the film just stumbles from one scene to the next in a very slapdash way. It's very poor at showing the passage of time which takes away from the toll the events are supposedly taking on the characters and I just wish the film let its scenes breathe more.

I don't really know that the script ever got to the heart of the relationship either, other than the barest of sensual chemistry. The means in which they get separated is massively contrived I never really felt like Fennell go me to root for the success of this relationship. The characters for the most part are horrifically unlikeable and in no interesting way. I enjoy characters with questionable morals but the script never pushes thrm into any interesting territory. The film starts with the conceit of 2 characters who feel they can't be together and it never truly evolves beyond that just making it very uninteresting as a story.

Margot Robbie gives a performance that to me is the prime example of the difference between bad and miscast. Because I can't call her acting bad here since she hits the right notes and conveys the right range of emotions but it never escaped the feeling that she just wasn't the right fit for this character. The character is someone who is childish and immature but being played by a fully grown woman makes her less redeemable in my eyes and in some ways underdeveloped. Jacob Elordi fares better because he has a bit more of a youthful energy but also a good amount of mysterious aura around him that suits this character so well. I just wish the script gave they more to work with because the more the script pits them against each other in increasingly diabolical ways the less I bought into their connection. The supporting cast doesn't fare much better because they're essentially boiled down to one or two core traits that make them feel more like plot devices than anything.

The visual spectacle on display though truly is something to behold and it was truly what kept me somewhat engaged throughout. Despite the writing being so weak it's clear that Fennell has a true passion for this story because of how she portrays it on a visual level. It's feels like there's a real attempt to do something visually interesting with each new scene and the use of colour can be breathtaking at points. Every single production and costume elements feels like it's produced in the highest possible level and it's always nice to see a film where its budget comes across on screen.

Wuthering Heights ends up as the first real disappointment of 2026 for me and something that just felt like a true waste of potential in every way. For as visually breathtaking as it is there's just nothing to latch onto on a writing level and as such nothing that's worth getting invested in. For as iconic as this story is nothing about the power it holds translates to screen in an impactful way. Emerald Fennell is still some I'll get excited about as a director but I just hope that she starts to evolve her storytelling technique more.

raw_payload.author: covaciaurora-41054

We don't need another Wuthering Heights adaptation if it's done this poorly.

raw_payload.author: wuytskristof-823-169489
raw_payload.content:
Went to an advance screening, really wished I didn't though...

This Wuthering Heights is less a gothic romance and more a lifeless dress rehearsal that somehow made it to theaters. The wild, obsessive passion that defines the novel is replaced with blank stares and hollow line readings. The moors look moodier than the cast.

Jacob Elordi proves once again that brooding is not the same as acting. Heathcliff is supposed to burn-Elordi barely flickers. His performance feels posed, polished, and completely detached from the character's raw brutality. Overrated doesn't even begin to cover it; this is charisma by algorithm.

And Robbie is no better: strained, theatrical in the worst way, mistaking intensity for volume. The chemistry between them is nonexistent, turning one of literature's most volatile love stories into an emotionally flat exchange.

A bleak, beautiful novel reduced to a glossy, empty shell. One star, and that's generous.

raw_payload.author: TheMovieSearch
raw_payload.content:
The 2026 adaptation of Wuthering Heights, starring Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi, presents itself as a sweeping romantic drama but leans heavily into sensuality as its primary storytelling device. Rather than focusing solely on emotional longing and tragic romance, the film frames much of its narrative through physical desire and provocative tension. It is undeniably adult in tone, using intimacy and lust as the driving force behind its character dynamics.

To its credit, the film is technically polished. The cinematography captures the moody atmosphere and windswept landscapes with elegance, creating a visually rich backdrop for the characters' volatile relationship. The direction maintains a steady, dramatic intensity, and the production design adds to the film's immersive quality. As a dramatic experience, it feels carefully constructed and visually refined.

However, the emphasis on sexualized tension often overshadows the emotional core of the story. Many scenes rely on suggestive behavior and explicit insinuation rather than layered dialogue or character development. While the film avoids graphic nudity, it repeatedly centers its narrative around physical dominance, humiliation, and raw desire. For some viewers, this approach may feel bold and passionate; for others, it may come across as excessive or distracting.

The performances are committed, particularly from Margot Robbie, who fully embraces the heightened emotional intensity of her character. Jacob Elordi delivers a physically imposing and brooding presence, especially in the film's earlier, rougher portrayal of his character. Yet as his character evolves into a more polished figure, the dynamic between the two sometimes feels visually uneven, making their shared history slightly harder to believe.

Ultimately, Wuthering Heights is a decent one-time watch for mature audiences who understand its tone and intentions. It tells its story through desire as much as dialogue, which will either resonate or alienate depending on the viewer. While it is competently made and occasionally compelling, the heavy reliance on sensuality limits its rewatch value. For those seeking a more traditional romantic drama rooted in emotional depth rather than physical tension, this version may feel more provocative than profound.

raw_payload.author: floatingdriftwood

As a full length adaptation of Wuthering Heights... it never gets off the ground.

raw_payload.author: Multifocus
raw_payload.content:
Why Wuthering Heights Endures :

Emily Brontë's Wuthering Heights is often underrated, yet it endures because of its emotional intensity, richly drawn characters, and vivid setting. In my view, it stands on the same level as a Shakespeare play-its passions, moral complexity, and tragic consequences are elemental and unforgettable. Beyond a simple love story, the novel explores multi-generational cycles of obsession, revenge, and toxic relationships, with the Yorkshire moors themselves acting as a living reflection of human turmoil. Its psychological depth, atmospheric power, and timeless insight give it an enduring resonance.

Why Film Adaptations Often Fall Short Most film adaptations flatten the story into a conventional romance, sacrificing its broader scope and emotional complexity for the demands of the screen. While movies can capture isolated moments of passion or tragedy, the novel's intricate layering of character, environment, and consequence creates a completeness that no adaptation has fully achieved-making Brontë's original far more powerful than any single film version.

The 2026 Adaptation : Emerald Fennell's latest adaptation, starring Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi, flattens the story even further. Where previous versions reduced the novel to romance, this film compresses that romance into a series of sexual encounters dressed up in lavish costumes and sumptuous cinematography. Lost entirely are the emotional intensity, moral complexity, and Shakespearean depth that make the original novel so enduring. What remains is a glossy, surface-level spectacle that mistakes eroticism for passion and visual excess for dramatic weight.

raw_payload.author: STaRSPRiNKLe_oX
raw_payload.content:
I haven't read the novel or seen any other adaptation of Brontë's work, so I went in with a pretty blank slate, zero expectation, and an open mind.

This movie was bleak, depressing, visually dark with lots of sex, and where you'd think that would hit the viewer, it did absolutely nothing. There were scenes clearly shot for shock value but they all just fell so flat. raw_payload.author: barnabaponchielli
raw_payload.content: Expectations were very low, I admit, but imagining that Fennel, after her praiseworthy, albeit imperfect, directorial debut, would dive into a kitsch delirium with a fetish for bodily fluids (she didn't skimp on stirring up the filth with Emily Brontë either...) without rhyme or reason ("Saltburn") and then take off towards a harmony filmed by Guillermo del Toro whose screenplay is entrusted to an army of ravenous but repressed monkeys, was the most unimaginable nightmare possible. The obscene treatment reserved for Emily Brontë's masterpiece proves that nothing sacred is left to win hearts numb from compulsive scrolling. But beautiful photographs meant to be used as memes aren't enough to win hearts, at least I sincerely hope so, because with "Wuthering Heights," we're already looking at the worst film of 2026 and the worst waste of time you can afford this year. Elordi looks like he stepped out of a requel of Del Toro's awful "Frankenstein", coincidentally, while Robbie is a cyborg who shines wherever you put her, regardless of the filth surrounding her (remember how disgusting Chazelle's "Babylon" was and how Margot shone regardless? Well, we're right in that ballpark, now that I think about it). "Wuthering Heights" is a colossal piece of crap, in short, and hopefully the last time Emerald Fennel is allowed to make a movie (which sadly won't happen...)!

raw_payload.author: constantinosisaias
raw_payload.content:
I walked into this adaptation of Wuthering Heights expecting to see the haunting, layered, tragic love story that has endured for generations. Instead, I left deeply disappointed.

This version, starring Margot Robbie and directed by Emerald Fennell, feels less like an adaptation and more like a reinvention. And that is precisely the problem.

Wuthering Heights is not just a passionate romance. It is a carefully structured, multi generational tragedy with emotional, psychological, and moral depth. The choices made in this film overly sexualize the story and reduce it to surface level intensity, stripping away the complexity that made the novel powerful in the first place. The original narrative does not need embellishment. It does not need modern sensationalism. Its brilliance lies in its restraint, its darkness, and its consequences.

What frustrates me most is the decision to change major elements of the story while still presenting it as an adaptation. If a filmmaker wants to tell a new story about obsessive love, then write a new script. But when adapting a literary classic, there is a responsibility to honor the architecture of the original work. This film feels like it borrows the title and characters but abandons the soul of the novel.

Wuthering Heights is a masterpiece without flaws in its design. Altering its structure and tone does not improve it. It diminishes it.

For those who love the book, this version may feel less like a tribute and more like a betrayal. I left not moved, but frustrated and saddened that such a timeless story was reshaped into something so unrecognizable.

This adaptation simply did not work for me.

raw_payload.author: hchmmyzv
raw_payload.content:
This is a bad film. The positives - Alison Oliver is such a good actor and she brings some welcome humour when she recounts Romeo and Juliet to Linton who in this film is her guardian. Martin Clunes is suitably grotesque and 'entertaining' as Cathy's father - EF has merged the characters of Earnshaw snr and jnr. The child actors do well - Owen cooper, Charlotte Mellington, and Vy Nguyen are all good. That's the good.

Questions: why does JE's Heathcliff call Cathy, Cath? Why does he spend a lot of the film standing like his character in Frankenstein? Why does he have an Elvis/beak pout for most scenes?

Why did Margot Robbie take on the role? Yes, she is a good actor but she is way too old for the role even with the added line from her father that she's left on the shelf.

The costumes look like they're out of C4's doc series Big Fat Gypsy Wedding.

The sets look like sets.

The dialogue is dull (apart from the few lines from Bronte's text) H: I love you C: I love you H: I love you cut to wide shot and then back to two shot H: I love you C: I love you... you get the idea... Where are the ghosts? Where are the layers of meaning?

I was so desperate for Cathy to hurry up and die. It wasn't sexy or passionate - it was dull.

raw_payload.author: dromasca
raw_payload.content:
A few years ago, when I watched 'Promising Young Woman', the debut feature film of English actress and screenwriter Emerald Fennell, I set out to follow the career of this promising filmmaker, who with that film won an Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay and also received nominations for Best Picture and Best Achievement in Directing. I did not see her second feature film, but now I have watched the third, the ambitious project that brings to the screen the troubled love story from the pages of Emily Brontë's novel 'Wuthering Heights'. As in all her films, Emerald Fennell is also the author of the script, which in this case is a screen adaptation of the novel. A very free adaptation, a real reinterpretation. In principle, I have no problem with that. It's just that too many things don't work or are not to my taste in this cinematic version of a book that has been brought to larger or smaller screens at least 21 times so far. It is, in my opinion, a spectacular and ambitious adaptation, which aims to present a different vision from the classic interpretations of the novel, but which lacks the social commentary and passionate authenticity of that story of a love relationship that destroys its heroes.

Those who have read the book or seen at least one of the film or television versions of the novel will recognize the main characters, but with small adaptations in essential places. Heathcliff seems less gloomy than I remember him, and Cathy seems much more mature, and therefore more evil, than the young woman just out of adolescence in the book. Their passion is present and well-founded in the childhood scenes (the best part of the film in my opinion) but the timid exchanges of glances and the falsely accidental touches in the novel are replaced by not very successful scenes of almost violent eroticism . The entire film seems marked by the cruelty of the opening scene, an execution by hanging that offers the children Cathy and Nelly (her companion maid) a spectacle that will mark their lives. Other characters from the book are not present in Emerald Fennell's version. This is the case of Cathy's brother, part of the character being taken over by her father. The next generation with its characters completely disappears, as does the prologue and epilogue that recall in the book the events thirty years after they happened.

Emerald Fennell wants viewers to understand that they are not watching an adaptation of Emily Brontë's novel so much as her personal vision of a story that is greater in purpose than the details related to the era in which the novel's action took place. The half-ruined house of the Earnshaws and the sumptuous estate of the Lintons acquire proportions, architectures, lights, shadows and colors that amplify the messages about the families that live in them. The screenwriter gave up any allusion to Heathcliff's race (which in the novel doubled the theme of class differences with the implicit one of racial differences), instead she cast Hong Chau - a Thai actress with Asian features that are hardly believable in the context of the era - in the role of Nelly (a pivotal character in the film as well, a kind of Polonius of this drama) and Shazad Latif in the role of Edgar. The outstanding acting performances however seemed to me to belong to Martin Clunes (delicious as Cathy's father) and to Owen Cooper, in his first role on the big screen, that of Heathcliff at young age. The young actor, after his formidable performance in the series 'Adolescence', seems to be building a career that could become that of a big star. The main roles bring together two of the most talented and successful actors of the moment. Margot Robbie is an actress that I liked in everything she did up to this film. She was terrific in Barbie, but now she's paying the price of that success and has to get rid of the image created by that film through roles that put less use of her wonderfully blue eyes and her physical beauty in general. In Jacob Elordi's filmography, Heathcliff from 'Wuthering Heights' comes after the role of the Creature in 'Frankenstein'. From the absolute monster to one of the male symbols of romantic love. His transition is fine, but the two actors didn't seem to me to have passionate and erotic tension on screen. Paradoxically, in a relatively bold film like this, it's precisely the love scenes between the two that are among the least successful. I respect the fact that Emerald Fennell aimed further than a simple cinematic adaptation, the film is in many moments beautiful in images and sound (especially when it uses Charli XCX's original songs), but the emotion and drama of the novel are not found on screen and have not been replaced with anything else.

raw_payload.author: pdonnelly-75330
raw_payload.content:
I went in with an open mind... but... this movie is terrible. If I could give it a zero I would.

The first of many big errors is the casting. Absurdly ridiculous! When a remake is made the proper casting is essential. Lawrence Olivier and Merle Oberon are surely rolling in their graves. Olivier did not even like Oberon and wanted his wife Vivienne Leigh cast, yet they were able to fulfill their roles to perfection.

The second of many big errors is not staying true to the time of the story. The anomalies are too numerous to list from hair styles to costumes to lighting.

It's really a shameless effort on Fennell's part. It's more about "look at me and my accomplishment" instead of remaining true to Brontë.

If you must watch, give it a few weeks until it is on tv and you'll be able to fast forward.

raw_payload.author: IvuskaG

raw_payload.author: harishnj
raw_payload.content:
This adaptation is a visually arresting film that unfortunately prioritizes style over the raw emotional depth of the source material. It is a good production in terms of technical craft but it fails to capture the true essence of the masterpiece by Emily Brontë. While it offers a striking cinematic experience it lacks the haunting psychological weight of the original novel.

Margot Robbie is stunning as Catherine Earnshaw. Her acting is the strongest element as she portrays a woman torn between social expectation and wild desire. However her performance is hamstrung by a screenplay that feels weak. The storytelling is fragmented trading gothic dread for marketable romance tropes and dialogue that feels out of place. It feels less like a faithful adaptation and more like a high budget music video. Purists will likely find it an empty reflection of the original work.

raw_payload.author: scotthodom
raw_payload.content: Classics are classics for a reason and don't need unnecessary and forgettable adaptations. "Wuthering Heights" is no different. I don't understand the fascination with the two leads here in Jacob Elordi and Margot Robbie. Neither one can carry a film and together the film just feels even more empty. Unfortunately, this won't be the last time a classic gets an unnecessary and ridiculous adaptation but people should vote with their wallets and skip nonsensical adaptations like this.

raw_payload.author: ambusched94
raw_payload.content:
Aside from the striking, modern-esque period piece costume design and some interesting cinematography shots in this film, this reimagining of novel Wuthering Heights tries too hard to be a passionate, sweeping love story that it falls very, very emotionally flat with unconvincing chemistry. In my opinion, Margot Robbie as Cathy and Jacob Elordi as Heathcliff have ZERO palpable on-screen chemistry, especially nonexistent, believable romantic chemistry. Sure, there was some lust between the characters but not hard to show the actors kissing each other, making out and groping, and displaying the lust chemistry, when it's so much more of a challenge to show two characters genuinely in love on-screen, which didn't come across as convincing in this film like AT ALL. I wasn't emotionally invested in their forbidden romance, didn't care about the obstacles keeping them apart, and wasn't sad when *spoiler alert* Cathy dies at the end from a miscarriage and Heathcliff is emotionally wrecked, mourning her dead body. Plain and simple, the two actors had no romantic chemistry and the film was overcompensating for what was missing. This will never be an enduring romance film.

On-screen chemistry is an elusive thing, it either exists or just doesn't. Look at the natural, captivating on-screen chemistry between the love interests in Titanic (1997), The Notebook (2004), Gone with the Wind (1939) and even in Spider-Man (2002), and compare it to the chemistry in this film, see there's no comparison at all. There's too much focus on the cinematography and the costume design, that they didn't even care about casting two actors who had believable on-screen romantic chemistry. Pass.

raw_payload.author: studioAT
raw_payload.content:
"From the director of 'Saltburn'. Ah, right, we knew what we were getting then.

This is 'Wuthering Heights' in name only, with Emerald Fennell (a woman who seems far too assured of her own ability) seemingly only dropping lines/bits of the original into the film as fan service.

It will get people through the doors into cinemas, but many will leave disappointed.

raw_payload.author: icminindy
raw_payload.content:
I was so excited to see the next adaptation of this amazing story after seeing the iconic Sir Lawrence Olivier and the amazing Merle Oberon 1939 movie which remains as one of the finest movies ever made. Granted, these were big shoes to fill, but certainly worthy of a remake/revision/adaptation.

As always in my reviews, I like to start off on the high notes. In this case; the talented Jacob Elordi does a great job with the material he's been given to work with. The camera loves him and the audience loves him right back. If there is a lul, it ends abruptly when Elordi appears on the screen. You might explain this because of his hauntingly good and interesting looks. However, you don't need to look any further than his recent incredible performance in Frankenstein to see that there is something much more about this actor and I beljve it transcends his physical beauty. This is a qualityt that few present day actors have and his acting talent is genuine and undenialble.

Hong Chau as Nelly is the other high note in this film. Her controlled emotions seathing beneath the surface are so well performed. Not over the top, not melodromatic, but just the perfect combination of inner resentment and discipline. Like Elorde, she consumes the screen each time she appears. The cinematography is nothing short or Oscar worthy.

Ok, here come my "but"s....... Set design was "interesting". Meaning, interesting to look at and creative. HOWEVER, in this film it appears out of place in time and mood to the point where it only serves as a distraction. It look like it belonged in a modern day stark horror film. It distracts from the story rather than add to it.

Margot Robbie as Cathy was lovely. However, someone, perhaps the writers, seemed to be content with skimming the surface of this charactor. She REACTS to siituations in each scence, but the charactorzation fails to dig deeper into what holds her back from expressing her love which often times seems inconsitent with her boldness in other scenes. My sense is that the flaw was not Margot Robbie's acting ability, but in either the writing or directing, or perhaps both. The same can be said in Act 2 where Heathcliff returns after his transition.

For some reason, this flaw did not appear to hold true for Hong Chau'a performance as Nelly or Shazad Latif's portrayal as Edgar (who is intentionally written as "uninteresting" to contrast the passion of Heathcliff). Alison Oliver delievers a solid performance as Isabella, but the depth of her charactor seemed to be abbreviated, perhaps in the interest of time, but what a missed opportunity that was!

In Conclusion: I felt that choices were made to make this epic opportunity as something exploitative of the two main characters instead of seizing the opportunity to have these talented actors create something beautiful and as timeless as the original, and perhaps even better. Where it not for Elordi and Hong Chau's performances, I would have rated this a two. A messsage to Elordi; don't let those who don't appreciate your talent steer you into forgetability. You have much more to offer and we all look forward to seeing it.

raw_payload.author: oanalarisat
raw_payload.content:
I really wanted to love this adaptation of Wuthering Heights, but it just felt hollow. It looks dramatic and intense on the surface, but there's barely any emotional depth underneath. The story jumps from one big moment to the next without actually building anything, so nothing really lands.

Heathcliff and Catherine are supposed to feel obsessive, tragic, almost mythic, here, they just feel inconsistent. One minute they're burning with passion, the next their choices make no sense. I never felt pulled into their psychology, and I couldn't bring myself to care about what happened to them.

At times, the transitions felt so abrupt that it was like watching a theatre play, scenes would shift without a natural cinematic flow, making everything feel staged instead of immersive.

It's all mood and gloom, but without the emotional backbone that makes the novel powerful. By the end, it felt heavy but strangely empty, like a storm with no thunder.

raw_payload.author: jhardie-41856
raw_payload.content: Emily Brontë is probably rolling over in her grave. Total smut. I don't mind artistic liberties in adaptations, but this wasn't even artfully done. Instead of inviting the audience to wrestle with the characters' moral complexity, their choices, revenge, and the uncomfortable truth that everyone sees themselves as the hero of their own story, the film is just a series of explicit scenes with little emotional build-up or psychological depth. What makes Wuthering Heights enduring is its exploration of obsession, pride, cruelty, and self-deception. Not shock value. This adaptation trades introspection for spectacle, flattening a layered narrative into something that feels anti-intellectual and hollow. There's a difference between bold storytelling and provocation for its own sake, and this unfortunately feels like the latter. Shaking my head. Beyond words disappointed that anyone would even put their name on this film and disrespect Bronte's legacy like this.

raw_payload.author: DoNotComeToTheCinemaDepressed
raw_payload.content:

Because of that.

I was Flat lined Throughout almost the Entirety Of this God Damn Film.

What an absolute Waste Of Time.

So Overall, It's an Awful Film, just Absolutely Awful.

raw_payload.author: chtsimone
raw_payload.content:
So, I didn't read the book, but after I went to see the movie (besides the fact that 2 days later, I went to see it again and I'm thinking of going AGAIN), the first thing I did was go buy the book, in its original English (which is not my first language) because the movie made me understand - I can't help but read this piece of art that somehow went past by me. And this is rare - making one to want to read the book AFTER they've watched the movie. I do see the reviews saying that the movie somehow disrespected the book but isn't that the point of an ADAPTATION (interpretation), and not an identical visual copy? Don't disregard my opinion because I don't know the original source material but take it as an honest opinion of someone who went in with no expectations and became enamoured with the movie and the story, instead.

Visually, I also fell in love with every shot and frame, which didn't surprise me at all because when I saw that Emerald Fennell would be the director, I knew it would be deliciously - truly - beautiful. I find the movie to be very underrated and overly criticised. But man, you can't mess with people's tastes. Do give it a chance.

raw_payload.author: thedesicritic
raw_payload.content:
Just for context, I have not read the book, so my only interaction with the source material is this movie. (Spoiler Alert)

Overview: This film had absolutely no depth and was a mess from beginning to end. Plot points were hinted at and then left unexplored, the performances were poor, roles were miscast, and there was no real message left for the audience. Regarding the depth of the film: what were the filmmakers trying to say? It was incredibly confusing, and there was no social commentary. Compare this to a film like Anna Karenina, which had far more depth in its characters and story while exploring similar themes. Wuthering Heights was like Anna Karenina but for unintelligent people.

The filmmakers left "Easter eggs," such as a hanging sequence that was given a lot of prominence but was never explored again. It made no sense for those scenes to be in the film, as they did not add to the storyline in any way. Another issue was the accumulation of wealth by Heathcliff (Jacob Elordi); the five years between his departure and return were completely unscrutinized. The audience was just meant to accept it-no questions asked. Additionally, the father (Martin Clunes) was shown as a violent and evil brute; we are never told why he chose to adopt Heathcliff, except for a throwaway one-line explanation. This went against every other choice he made in the movie.

There was no moral message in the movie at all. By the end, we were not sympathetic toward any of the characters, so it was confusing what the message was meant to be.

Plot: The story follows Cathy (Margot Robbie) and Heathcliff, two children who grow up together in different financial circumstances. From their childhood feelings for one another, life takes them in different directions. They reunite as adults and begin an affair.

Acting: Margot Robbie: She performed adequately, but in my opinion, she was a total miscast considering the script. The script was obviously written with a younger actress in mind-someone who would have married in their late teens or early twenties. When listening to Cathy's tantrums and histrionics, it was clear that the actress was meant to be someone on the cusp of girlhood and womanhood. Margot Robbie, at 35, just did not fit the role.

Jacob Elordi: A truly awful performance. He basically had one expression the entire film. A total miscast, he lacked any sort of charisma or screen presence.

The supporting characters all delivered decent and acceptable performances.

Genre: A quick Google search will tell you that Wuthering Heights is set in the late 18th century. Part of being a period piece is respecting the era. This movie fails to do that by casting ethnic minorities in major roles, which takes the audience out of the story. While it is a minor point, it ruins the illusion. In the historical fiction genre, the history matters as much as the fiction.

Conclusion: I have very little positive feedback for this film. Despite having great source material and a compelling story, the execution was far too disorganized for my liking.

3/10.

raw_payload.author: petermadsen-65702
raw_payload.content:
A good looking movie with two attractive and talented leads and decent instruction by Emerald Fennell, but alas, this unfaithful, lustful adaption of the classic novel from Emily Brontë is "50 Shades of Wuthering Heights".

Devoid of any soul and plenty of spice, it's a 10/10 movie perfect for Generation BookTok.

raw_payload.author: FR-Foster
raw_payload.content:
The root of this dark and horrific story is a family destroyed by a physically and mentally abusive alcoholic father. In the original book it was the brother. In this movie it's the toxic patriarch - highlighted by the scene of his death with bottles piled high in the corners of the room, after he's gambled away the family fortune. Under this lens the sadistic, selfish acts of Heathcliff and Cathrine (and Nellie) are all a byproduct of their upbringing, especially clear for those of us who were raised in addict/alcoholic families. This is anything but a love story.

Beautifully shot, and incredible performances from the cast, but a heart breaking and deeply disturbing experience. Can't think of a good reason to recommend to anyone. Absolutely awful that the producers thought it a good idea to release for Valentines.

raw_payload.author: KarenM-6286
raw_payload.content: Hands up, I love the novel. Hands down one of the worst films I have endured. The majesty of the Yorkshire "Wuthering Heights" reduced to a mere backdrop for ghastly theatrical costumes. Catherine looking like she might start yodelling but lacking the passion to bother. Zero chemistry between the leads who are both too old for the roles. All styling and no substance. I felt nothing. The novel made me sob. Please God don't let the director get her hands on Jane Eyre.

raw_payload.author: stephiearan
raw_payload.content:
Did it disrespect the source material? A book I love? Yes, absolutely. Did I tremendously enjoy the set design, the costumes, the cinematography, the MUSIC, the acting from both leads, and everything that Isabella did and said? ALSO YES. Sue me.

Knowing that it is not a faithful adaptation going into the movie actually allowed me to view them as two separate pieces.

For people who are upset by this version, go watch one of the dozen more faithful adaptations.

raw_payload.author: cedricdumler
raw_payload.content:
The sets, the cinematography, the overall aesthetic are gorgeous to look at - that's what you get when you have Linus Sandgren as your DOP (can't wait for his work in Dune 3) - but everything else is a disaster. Emerald Fennell completely misunderstands the source material, stripping away all of Brontë's social critique, class commentary, and the darker truths of abuse to deliver a teen-fantasy version that is visually polished but empty inside. The story's depth, the societal pressures, the stakes are gone.

Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi try to bring life to their characters, and you can see glimpses of it, but the script gives them nothing to work with. Heathcliff is no longer the complex, haunted outsider; Cathy is no longer navigating love, survival, or family duty. Motivations barely exist and emotional stakes are flattened into clichés. Watching talented actors trapped in this mess is painful.

Visually stunning, narratively empty. Fennell may have nailed the Pinterest-perfect aesthetic, but she utterly fails to capture the heart of the story. Cold, shallow, and forgettable.

Belongs to the category of pretentious cinema.

3/10.

raw_payload.author: kanayamegido
raw_payload.content: Even if we completely ignore the source material and view this movie as a stand-alone piece of media, there is nothing redeeming about this film. It is a movie made by a fake freak who stands for nothing and says nothing profound despite desperately wanting to seem deep and influential. It was made for people who like to "turn their brains off" and scroll on twitter while listening to a sub-par podcast on their headphones while a bland and hollow film plays in the background so they can say they technically watched that film and give it ten stars on rotten tomatoes. This movie is the physical embodiment of everything wrong with visual forms of media in the modern day; focusing on visuals instead of storytelling and valuing spectacle and shock over an actual plot. It insults the intelligence of those who watch it and it insults the work, memory, and legacy of Emily Brontë, may she haunt Emerald Fennell indefinitely.

raw_payload.author: parksiet
raw_payload.content:
Some stories are beautiful because they hurt.

"Wuthering Heights" is exactly that type of story.

Emerald Fennell's take on Emily Brontë's novel is so beautifully made, so singular in vision, that I was kind of in awe of it. This is a bold, gothic visually vibrant interpretation - one that absolutely takes significant liberties (hence the quotations around the title), but as someone who isn't a die-hard purist of the source material, I loved how much Fennell experimented. I'm always in support of reinterpretation, creativity, and artists boldly trying something new. "Wuthering Heights" is Fennell taking the title, framework, and essence of the original novel and building off it to create an ode to doomed romances with some unique and thoughtful insight.

And doomed it is.

I loved the emotion, the yearning, the tragedy of it all. For most of the film, I was completely swept away by the intoxicating romance - the aching longing between Heathcliff and Cathy, and the way their souls seem to mirror each other so deeply. You feel their desire, their longing for each other. It's steamy, wild, sensual, the kind of big, overwhelming romantic drama that cinema has been missing for a while. And then the third act pulls back the curtain. What once felt sweeping and romantic reveals itself to be obsessive, toxic, and destructive. And my god, did the tragedy of it all ever make me cry by the end!

Genuinely gave me the best cry I've had in a while! Any film that can make me full on ugly cry deserves a full 5 stars!

The passion, desire and heartbreak are as vivid and overwhelming as the striking design choices crafted by Fennell, cinematographer Linus Sandgren, production designer Suzie Davis, and costume designer Jacqueline Durran. I love the aesthetic, atmosphere and style Fennell and her team brought to the film. Visually, this is a breathtaking work of art. Shot in VistaVision, the cinematography has this gorgeous, classic texture that immediately evokes the grand epics of David Lean; I was reminded of Doctor Zhivago and Great Expectations, but with a twist of Moulin Rouge. There are even shades of Gone with the Wind and Romeo and Juliet in the way Fennell frames this as a grand, destructive love story. The dark lighting, the mist rolling over the moors, the slick camera movements, the framing and blocking of each shot - it's stunning and creates such an awesomely gothic atmosphere. There are some shots that immediately stuck out to me as some of my favourite in recent cinema! The production design is impeccable, the sets gorgeously crafted with such immense detail that adds to the story, and my god the costumes are immaculate. It pays homage to old Hollywood with the lush and beautiful costumes and actual sets, with no noticeable over-reliance on CGI. I was almost overwhelmed by the spectacle of it all.

I also loved the tonal balance. There's a rich gothic darkness here, but also a titillating romantic energy that pulses throughout the film. And what really struck me was how dirty and lived-in the world of it felt. We so often romanticize the world of period dramas - think Pride & Prejudice or Sense and Sensibility, even Bridgerton to an extent - but this embraces something more period accurate and grimy. The opening scene did a fantastic job of letting us as the audience get acquainted with a world and story that wasn't going to be so picturesque, which builds into the toxic relationship we begin to follow.

Performance-wise, Margot Robbie is brilliant as usual, fully committing to Cathy's volatility and yearning. But it's Jacob Elordi who overwhelmed me. His brooding, lustful presence is magnetic - devastatingly electric, passion dialed past excess. Together, their chemistry and sexual tension is so intense you can practically cut it with a knife. They sell the ecstasy and the despair of loving someone who may ultimately destroy you. And Alison Oliver quietly emerges as a secret weapon in the ensemble.

The music elevates everything even further. Anthony Willis's sweeping score paired with Charli XCX's songs creates this intoxicating, surging atmosphere of longing and chaos. It's bold, overheated, unruly - gloriously uninterested in subtlety. I was surprised at how well the modern musical elements worked with the period drama of the film, I ended up loving how they were used. Much like the music, everything in the film comes together in an intense sensation of longing, ecstasy and despair. Messy and overlong as it may be, the romantic in me, who likes his emotions big, fell hard for it.

And for those expecting something freaky or as provocatively shocking as Saltburn - I was genuinely impressed by the level of restraint Fennell shows here, particularly in how she approaches the story's sexual elements. She lets the dialogue, the tension, and our imagination do most of the work. It's far more effective and emotionally powerful that way. By comparison, some of the sexual moments/elements in Saltburn felt driven more by shock value and an intent to provoke or divide audiences whereas this feels focused - intense, yes, but purposeful.

Emerald Fennell goes three for three for me. This might be her most luminously feral film yet - a love story that doesn't glow so much as burn. It feels like a major awards contender, especially in cinematography, costume design, production design, and score. Audiences are going to be floored by what she's achieved here, and "Wuthering Heights" firmly cements her as one of my favourite directors working today.

An epic, yearning romance with a beautiful, uncompromising vision. Steamy, emotional, captivating, and tragic.

Some stories are beautiful because they hurt.

This one hurt in all the right ways.

raw_payload.author: eddie_baggins
raw_payload.content:
It's entirely plausible that a large portion of Wuthering Heights keen audience are heading into a film they expect to be a certain something that it's absolutely not, as writer/director Emerald Fennell marches headfirst through her very loose adaptation of Emily Brontë's classic that was first published in the late 1840's.

Working Brontë's text into her own version of a seemingly doomed romance between Margot Robbie's prim and proper Catherine Earnshaw and Jacob Elordi's orphaned servant boy she names Heathcliff, with the two characters also portrayed impressively by Charlotte Mellington and Adolescence breakout star Owen Cooper respectively in their younger years, Fennell will offend and annoy many with her version of this beloved tale but at the same time she will win many over with her fiercely passionate and artistic take on a frequently told story.

Bringing the same energy and vibrancy she delivered in her Oscar nominated debut Promising Young Woman and even more so in her divisive and much talked about sophomore feature Saltburn, from the moment Heights starts in an unexpected and eye-popping fashion there's little stopping it across its two hour plus running time, even if the films much fabled controversies end up being mostly pre-release hype as Fennell shows some forms of restraint in doing/showing things to shock and titillate, with nothing here coming close to the likes of Saltburn's bathtub or graveyard sequences.

A melting pot of unrealized love, passion, friendship and soul searching, as well as some unexpectedly hearty doses of comedic relief at times, courtesy mostly of Martin Clune's reimagined Mr. Earnshaw and Alison Oliver's vulnerable Isabella, Heights is a lot of things all at once and can at times falter in balancing out its many ingredients but it's all very minor when Fennell and her two leads are so committed to the cause.

Backed by Linus Sandgren's eye-capturing cinematography, another fine collaboration between himself and Fennell after Saltburn, and an emotive score by Anthony Willis that works in well with Charlie XCX's songs, the on-screen chemistry and back and forward between Robbie and Elordi will most likely go down as some of the years most talked about and after a hefty pre-release campaign blitz by the two talented Australian ex-pats, it's great to see it wasn't all bark and no bite.

Full of longing looks, soulful gazes and more than a few kisses, this iteration of Catherine and Heathcliff might not stick excessively to the original text, but it doesn't stop this exploration of their affections from being any less effective, as young friends turned complicated soul mates plays out before our eyes, sure to affect anyone who has bought into Fennell's vision that makes no apologies for being the way it is.

Three films into her feature film career, there appears to be no slowing down for Fennell, who by now has showed enough to be regarded as one of the most creative and unapologetic writer/directors out there, even if her style and her approach isn't going to win over any new fans as she goes merrily on her way no matter the kickback.

Final Say -

This is not the Wuthering Heights you grew up with but Fennell's take on the classic has crafted its own unique vibrancy and heart, one that's delivered memorably by a fiery cast and a creative aesthetic that's going to be divisive but beautiful and unexpected for those that jump on board.

4 1/2 broken eggs out of 5.

raw_payload.author: cestmoi-29203
raw_payload.content:
Artsy? Only if slow-motion hair flips now qualify as high cinema.

Faithful to the novel? In the same way a Halloween costume is faithful to history.

Entertaining? If your idea of entertainment is watching a two-hour perfume commercial masquerading as literature.

So what was it? A glossy, self-satisfied rebrand of Wuthering Heights that remembers the names and forgets everything else.

Let's be honest: this is Heathcliff™ - The Premium Edition. The camera doesn't just film Jacob Elordi; it worships him. Every beam of light, every lingering close-up, every carefully staged entrance screams: Do not look away. Cathy (Margot Robbie) may have the gowns and screen time, but Heathcliff has the devotion. This isn't storytelling - it's cinematic thirst.

And it works. When Heathcliff returns to Wuthering Heights clean-shaven and tailored, the theater practically gasps in unison. Mission accomplished. The target demographic has been successfully identified and serviced.

As for character development? Optional. Psychological complexity? Inconvenient. The film borrows from Emily Brontë the way influencers borrow vintage books for aesthetic shelf décor - visible spine, unread content. The moral savagery, the emotional brutality, the generational damage - all politely airbrushed away to make room for brooding stares and romanticized torment.

The novel was never a swooning love story. It was feral, toxic, and corrosive. This adaptation insists on reframing it as tragic romance, as if the audience might faint without a steady drip of desirability. The result feels less like interpretation and more like dilution.

And then come the visual name-drops - a dash of Gone with the Wind, a hint of Dangerous Liaisons, all filtered through music-video editing that mistakes fragmentation for sophistication. Instead of coherence, we get mood. Instead of depth, we get styling. Instead of tragedy, we get aesthetic longing.

In the end, it's not offensive enough to be memorable, nor profound enough to matter. It's prestige packaging around narrative emptiness - handsome, calculated, and utterly forgettable.

Except, of course, for Heathcliff's cheekbones.

raw_payload.author: Jelisei
raw_payload.content:
What a horrible mom's porn. Was this supposed to be serious? It feels less like an adaptation and more like a parody of over-dramatized, over-sexualized, over-romantic melodrama. I had to actively stop myself from laughing every five minutes. It's embarrassing. Cringe. Completely fake.

That scene with Elordi riding off and then heroically turning back against a blood-red sunset? I mean... come on. Or when Catherine's dad dies and they literally stack not one but two (!) huge piles of bottles behind him like, "Look! He was an alcoholic!" Thanks, I got it. Subtlety died long before he did.

The movie itself is just badly done. The story is told in a messy, chaotic way. The acting feels like parody. Elordi and Robbie are good actors - but here? Unwatchable. Completely unbelievable. They're supposed to be the same age (if anything, he should be older), yet there's a visible gap. Margot - though she looks great - clearly looks older than her partner, and it only makes everything feel more artificial.

And the whole "we'll wait until our 40s to act on this" logic makes zero sense. Two teenagers living together, obsessed with each other, with no one else around - and they just... wait decades? Really?

Fake, fake, fake. Instead of tragic passion, it's glossy hysteria.

raw_payload.author: grinningelvis
raw_payload.content: It doesn't seem as if Edgelord Emerald Fennell has actually read Wuthering Heights, let alone thought about it very much. That's fine so long as your film isn't called "Wuthering Heights" s manages to be vaguely watchable. If there's sex here at all it's being delivered by two actors pushing 40 playing teens pretending to actually have emotions and something like physical attraction. It's stunningly awful, and not in a so-bad-it's-good way. The film has nothing to say and seemingly no one to say it. Dead on arrival and really pretty embarrassing, it probably could have been improved by a second run through ChatGPT. Hard to believe Robbie read this script and said "that's my next film."

raw_payload.author: jibiliana
raw_payload.content: If you have read any Brontë do not even consider going to this movie. It bears no resemblance to anything written by the women who have been celebrated authors for years, in fact it's an insult to literature. This is basically pornography, in period costume in bad weather (because then you can see through Margot Robbie's nightgown!) How ridiculous and pathetic.

raw_payload.author: Boxer_007
raw_payload.content:
I've never read the book, and I wasn't expecting or demanding a faithful adaptation. I went in open-minded, ready to judge this purely as a film on its own terms.

Visually, it's stylish and polished, with a modern soundtrack that clearly aims for "prestige-meets-TikTok-era cool." Unfortunately, that's where most of the praise ends.

As a romance, it completely falls flat. The two leads are attractive, sure, but there's almost no chemistry, no real buildup, no tension. It feels like watching that good-looking but strangely empty couple from high school. You're told this is a grand, obsessive love story, but you never really feel it.

It's also not erotic. There's barely any real sensuality, no genuine daring, and what's there comes off more like awkward, half-committed provocation than actual heat. A bit of cheap lust, some odd fetishized framing, but nothing that feels bold, intimate, or emotionally charged.

And as a tragedy, it's oddly anticlimactic. Because the central relationship never convinces, the emotional payoff just isn't there. Add to that a disjointed second half with messy pacing, and instead of being gripped or devastated, I found myself mostly waiting for it to end.

In the end, this feels less like a serious attempt at timeless storytelling and more like a "TikTok medieval novel" engineered for aesthetics, social media chatter, and marketing buzz rather than substance. I'm sure it'll be loved by people who already enjoy that kind of glossy, vibe-first content.

raw_payload.author: euro_xo
raw_payload.content: In summary: Frankenstein (2025) meets Poor Things (2023) meets Pride & Prejudice (2005). Forget everything you know of Brontë's source material for a second and just embrace the film as it is: a visually beautiful film presenting a tragic love story. The casting is superb from the talented young actors playing Catherine & Heathcliff (Owen Cooper literally won an Emmy for Adolescence, for crying out loud!), to the electric chemistry between Margot Robbie & Jacob Elordi. The yearning... THE YEARNING! You have no choice but to embrace the reinterpretation as is - and once you do, it is truly stunning. Give the film a chance, I promise it'll break your heart (in a good way).

raw_payload.author: PerryAtTheMovies
raw_payload.content:
5.7/10 (Vaguely Recommend)

The film tries to be a tad bit Fifty Shades of the Georgian Age while at the same time it tries to be some sort of story you can care about. What I mean by this is that it tries to be a steamy, sensual romance, but leaves one with more desire. While it may wish for you to feel the love and twine of two individuals, it feels more like to two adults just wanting to hook up with no genuine emotion between them. Steam isn't the only thing that lacks. The drama is also not really there. While it exists in bits and pieces, it is poorly executed and lacks the actual drama that would've made things enjoyable.

While this story may be good on paper in its original book form, the film in and of itself leaves much to be desired. There's no care for, or emotional bond between any of the characters. Despite Margot Robbie's seeming ability to cry on cue, there's very little enjoyable acting going on. These are good actors that have done well in other films, but their chemistry with each other just isn't there.

The few good parts are the cinematography, the wardrobe, and the score. The way some parts are captured is enjoyable. I actually really enjoyed the wardrobe for Jacob Elordi, but mainly Margot Robbie's dresses. They captured the era well. I loved the score at times. It was the only thing that gave me emotional enjoyment.

Overall, I would not recommend seeing this one in theatres even on a cheap night. It seriously lacks any emotional undertones. Laughing can be had throughout, but no love, happiness, sadness, or even disappointment is reciprocated. Steam and drama are nearly non-existent. The actors are good elsewhere, but not here. Their chemistry generally sucks.

That's it for now. Thanks for taking time to read my review. Happy watching and enjoy the show!

raw_payload.author: fabiolpinheiro1993
raw_payload.content:
For a film called Wuthering Heights, it certainly is full of lows.

It may have been a groundbreaking story when Wuthering Heights was published in 1847, and it remains one of the most famous novels - and tragic love stories - of all time. But in 2026, this adaptation feels painfully dated, overdone, and tired. We've seen this story a million times. The forbidden love trope has been recycled endlessly, and this version brings absolutely nothing new to the table. No fresh nuance, no deeper social commentary - just a plain, predictable, boring and ultimately uninteresting narrative.

Technically, the film is strong. The dramatic and provocative visuals are striking. The costume design and score are bold choices, deliberately contrasting with the time period. While not revolutionary, they are at least interesting enough to make the film stand out stylistically.

But style can't compensate for emotional emptiness.

The film constantly tells you what to feel and why you should care - yet it never earns those emotions. It's nearly impossible to connect with the story or invest in what's happening. The characters are deeply unlikable, and by the end, you may find yourself actively disliking nearly everyone.

The cast is undeniably impressive. Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi are two of the most charismatic actors working today. But not even they can make these characters compelling. Worse still, they share virtually no on-screen chemistry - a fatal flaw for a romance built entirely on obsessive passion.

Character development is practically nonexistent. Characters disappear for years and return drastically changed, with little to no explanation. Motivations are underexplored. Emotional arcs feel rushed or abandoned altogether. The writing feels misguided and uneven, and the abrupt, illogical ending only amplifies the frustration.

In the end, Wuthering Heights is technically beautiful but emotionally hollow - an unnecessary adaptation that mistakes aesthetic ambition for substance, and ultimately feels like a waste of time.

raw_payload.author: SAMTHEBESTEST
raw_payload.content:
Wuthering Heights (2026) : Movie Review -

I am probably one of those rare people who genuinely dislike Wuthering Heights. Even though William Wyler is one of my all-time favorite filmmakers, I have always held a grudge against him for making Wuthering Heights (1939). That said, a couple of Hindi adaptations have worked better for me. Although I never adored the original source material, Jugnu (1947) and Dillagi (1949) felt far more engaging because of the changes they made. Many '90s viewers would remember Dhadkan (2000), which borrowed from Wuthering Heights and altered the climax to suit our sensibilities. After all these adaptations, I finally got to watch another modern take on the story-only to dislike it yet again, despite its lavish presentation and updated treatment.

I won't recount the plot since almost everyone already knows it. Instead, I'd rather focus on what bothers me: the characters and their choices. For me, it's a tale of two foolish lovers who consistently make the wrong decisions and then spend their lives drowning in regret. They don't just err once-they repeat their mistakes endlessly, corrupting something as pure as love. Heathcliff once says, "I won't play with you anymore," and for a fleeting moment, you admire his maturity. But from there on, he behaves immaturely for the rest of his life. Catherine chooses Edgar, fully aware of what she's doing-so why blame Nelly later? She had already made her decision, and Heathcliff would have left regardless. What difference could she truly have made? Isabella knowingly steps into disaster and then becomes almost like his pet-are we really meant to sympathize? Her behavior feels childish and irrational. Edgar, too, keeps himself trapped in a loveless situation. For a wealthy and intelligent man, his choices feel painfully naive and foolish. And Heathcliff-did he have nothing else to do in life? Did he earn enough in five years to survive on vengeance forever?

This modern version is far darker and far more intense than the original. It contains several explicit and excessively intimate moments. In 1939, Wyler was restricted by the movie code; explicit sexual content was impossible. In 2026, however, such scenes seem almost mandatory, especially for a Valentine's weekend release. So why would this film hold back? There are moments showing Margot Robbie pleasuring herself, Jacob Elordi tasting her finger afterward, multiple passionate kisses, and Alison Oliver baring herself in several scenes. The first half maintains a solid grip, but the second half becomes exhausting-just as the story has always been for me. Heath and Cath's repetitive emotional chaos drains the energy until her death finally arrives. Technically, though, there are some beautifully romantic scenes. One striking image I won't forget is a husband painting a wall to match the blush of his wife's cheek.

As for performances, the cast does well. Margot Robbie looks stunning and handles the demanding arc impressively. Jacob Elordi is convincingly charming at first and then terrifying as his character spirals into rage. Alison Oliver fits naturally into her unconventional role, while Hong Chau makes every moment count despite limited dialogue. Martin Clunes and Ewan Mitchell are adequate in their parts. Shazad Latif is another notable performer in the lott.

The cinematography shines in select sequences, though a few scenes feel poorly executed. The VFX is underwhelming. The background score supports even the dull stretches, and the production design is serviceable. However, the editing and pacing are major issues. The second half truly tests your patience. Since most viewers already know the story, you could technically walk out midway and miss nothing essential. Emerald Fennell gives the film a soft, romantic sheen, which might appeal to die-hard fans. But if you're looking for intelligent storytelling or layered characters, this is far from satisfying and decent cinema. Rather, it's indecent I'd say. Thankfully, it wasn't unbearably dull, and it didn't completely ruin my mood, so I managed to sit through the 135 minutes. You could watch it with your partner and perhaps be swept away by its passion. But if either of you expects depth or sensible writing - she or you can say, "We are doomed."

RATING - 4/10*

raw_payload.author: steiner-sam
raw_payload.content:
It's a romantic tragedy beginning in 1771 in the Yorkshire Moors. Catherine Earnshaw (Charlotte Mellington/Margot Robbie) is the only child of an abusive and alcoholic father (Martin Clunes). Her only friend is attendant Nelly Dean (Vy Nguyen/Hong Chau). One day, Mr. Earnshaw brings home a young silent boy whom Catherine dubs Heathcliff (Owen Cooper/Jacob Elordi). They have a close, intense relationship. Some years later, wealthy merchant Edgar Linton (Shazad Latif) moves next door with his ward, Isabella (Alison Oliver). They have a pompous house, and Catherine becomes interested in escaping her poverty. The film follows the relationship of Catherine, Heathcliff, Edgar, and Isabella through the next six years.

Emerald Fennell's "Wuthering Heights" turns Emily Brontë's story into a bodice-ripper. She eliminates major characters (Hindley and Hareton), and changes the character of others (Joseph). The opening is a bizarre creation, and the relationship between Catherine and Heathcliff is boiled down to sexuality. None of the novel's complexity is retained. Many strange posed sets become distracting. Margot Robbie seems a bit old for the Catherine role. This 2026 version is much inferior to the 2011 British drama.

raw_payload.author: joana-niza

I knew it was not supposed to be a faithful retelling of Emily Brontë's novel but a new and creative "adaptation" of it.

So I went to the theatre open minded and tried to set the book aside and judge the film on its own terms. But that proved nearly impossible since the movie had nothing new to offer or say or it didn't know what it was trying to say; It was shallow, it had no depth, it had no substance... And you can't help but compare it with the novel that goes by the same name and, in contrast to it, has so much to say!

In my perspective, an adaptation has to preserve at least the original message of the story, the driving conflict, the central themes, the pivotal moments, characterisation, etc. This movie though, strays so far from Wuthering Heights novel that calling it an "adaptation" feels almost generous. I feel that it was called that way just for marketing purposes, to get buzz and get those box office numbers... and that decision just brought down the movie even more.

I found myself more often frustrated than engaged, and honestly frequently bored.

Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi failed to make me connect and feel the intensity in Heathcliff and Catherine's relationship, which made the intended emotional impact of the ending fall completely flat.

Emerald Fennell's "Wuthering Heights" ultimately feels less like an interpretation and more like a misreading.

On a positive note, i enjoyed Alison Oliver's performance as Isabella (of course forgetting the real Isabella's character and arc in the book which would have been way more interesting if portrayed in the movie); i liked the production and costume designs and Charli XCX soundtrack surprisingly works, carrying a lot of the movie's emotional weight and being one of the strongest elements of this film.

Nonetheless, this movie was not it for me.

Soundtrack - Pretty Good: Charli XCX's talents are wasted for this movie because, although the songs are good, they are just misused for this train wreck; I also am not a huge fan of when they use contemporary music in period pieces

Cinematography - Very Good: The movie is beautifully shot to show the vast world these characters occupy and the stunning sets they live on, but this is something you would expect from a big-budget movie like this; It is such a shame Sandgren's talents were wasted on this movie because it genuinely is some of his best work

Editing - Good: The movie is well-edited and put together; It works in conjunction with the cinematography to make this movie feel like a visually stunning period piece, which is a shame because the editor's talents were really wasted for this movie

Production Design - Very Good: The sets for this movie were beautifully designed, as they did create an aesthetic world that is well-crafted, but this is what you would expect from a big-budget movie like this

Makeup - Pretty Bad: The aging makeup was really bad for this movie because it did not look like these characters aged at all

Costumes - Good: The costumes are well-designed, as they add to the film's aesthetic even though they may be incredibly culturally inaccurate; These costumes being beautifully designed is what you would expect from a big-budget movie like this

Pacing - Pacing is bad because this movie just feels incredibly slow, which is most likely due to the movie just being hard to watch with how bad it is; Even if they cut ten minutes from Acts 2 and 3, this movie would have still been bad

Climax - The climax artificially forces you to care about what is going on, but by this point, I was just wishing for this movie to end

Tone - The tone plays like erotic fan fiction of the English Romanticism and Gothic periods, mixed with contemporary elements to make me just hate everything about this movie

Final Notes - I was visibly mad for two hours watching this movie.

raw_payload.author: superally-79442
raw_payload.content:
One of the most astonishing miscalculations of the 2026 adaptation of Wuthering Heights is its apparent conviction that explicit degradation equals depth.

It does not.

In Brontë's novel, sexuality is largely implied, submerged beneath social codes and volcanic restraint. That restraint is precisely what makes the emotional intensity unbearable. Catherine and Heathcliff's bond feels dangerous because it is spiritual, possessive, metaphysical. Their obsession is not about bodies colliding. It is about identities dissolving. When Catherine says she is Heathcliff, it is existential horror, not foreplay.

The book's power lies in repression. In silence. In glances that carry the weight of entire futures collapsing. The violence is psychological, economic, generational. Heathcliff degrades people through control, inheritance, humiliation, and calculated cruelty. The sadism is systemic and slow-burning.

The film, by contrast, appears to have taken the word "dark" and interpreted it as "let's make this aggressively explicit."

Instead of letting obsession simmer in the marrow, it externalizes everything into scenes that feel less like Gothic tragedy and more like a director determined to prove how fearless they are. The sex is not merely passionate. It is often framed as degrading, transactional, power-laden in ways that scream for attention rather than illuminate character. There is a difference between portraying toxicity and aestheticizing it.

In the novel, degradation is emotional and social. Catherine humiliates Heathcliff by choosing Edgar for status. Heathcliff retaliates by dismantling entire family lines. That is cruelty with architecture.

In the film, degradation risks becoming spectacle. Bodies are displayed. Power is made literal. Sadism is rendered in a way that feels designed to provoke headlines rather than deepen understanding. It is the difference between a wound that festers unseen and a neon sign flashing "THIS IS EDGY."

And here is the real betrayal.

Brontë's work is disturbing because it refuses titillation. It makes you complicit in observing something morally rotten. You feel uncomfortable not because of nudity, but because of emotional annihilation. The characters' intimacy is frightening because it is possessive and spiritually claustrophobic.

When a modern adaptation foregrounds pornographic intensity, it shifts the axis. The audience is no longer unsettled by metaphysical obsession. They are simply confronted with explicitness. Shock replaces dread. Sensation replaces tragedy.

It is rather like adapting King Lear and deciding the true heart of the play is how vigorously everyone argues while partially undressed.

The original understands that cruelty in love is about control, pride, and revenge. It is about Catherine choosing status over soul and Heathcliff spending decades turning that rejection into a weapon. The sexual undercurrent is powerful precisely because it is restrained, because Victorian society forces it into shadow.

Drag it into full glare, make it graphic and sadistic for visual emphasis, and you flatten the horror into something far less interesting.

Brontë wrote about souls clawing at each other across social barriers and death itself.

The film sometimes appears to believe the real tragedy is insufficient intensity in the bedroom.

One is a study of obsession so profound it corrodes generations.

The other risks mistaking degradation for profundity.

And if you cannot tell the difference between existential ruin and provocative choreography, perhaps the moors were never the problem.
