raw_payload.author: kevin_robbins
raw_payload.content:
My wife and I watched Wuthering Heights (2026) in theaters. The storyline follows a young lady and an orphan raised in poverty who are inseparable and share an unspoken love. When a wealthy tradesman moves in next door, the young lady accepts his proposal for marriage, setting off a series of tragic events and creating a love triangle defined by pain and suffering.

This picture is directed by Emerald Fennell (Saltburn) and stars Margot Robbie (Barbie), Jacob Elordi (Euphoria), Hong Chau (The Whale), and Shazad Latif (Penny Dreadful).

This movie far exceeded my expectations in just about every way. The production is top-notch. The depiction of the era is exceptional-from the settings, props, sets, attire, hairstyles, and décor-it's easy to get pulled into its universe. The poor feel genuinely poor and hopeless, and the wealthy feel authentically privileged. The contrast in lifestyles is portrayed extremely well.

The storytelling combined with the acting is fantastic. The child actors who open the film set the tone perfectly. Robbie and Elordi play off each other beautifully, and the supporting cast is phenomenal and captivating. The Isabella character, in particular, is outstanding and worth the price of admission alone.

The movie unfolds very well, and the background music complements it perfectly. The shots of the countryside are stunning, and every interior room is captured with precision and intention. The film's final climactic overhead shots of the bed are elite artistry. Even smaller moments, like the pig-carving scene and the violent confrontation scenes, are brilliantly executed. The cinematography and presentation elevate everything. The way the story ultimately comes together at the end is powerful. There's a lot to appreciate here.

In conclusion, Wuthering Heights is a perfect roller coaster of tragedy and romance, unfolding simultaneously toward a tumultuous and conclusion. I'd score this a 9/10 and strongly recommend it.

raw_payload.author: darkreignn
raw_payload.content:
"Wuthering Heights" is perhaps best defined by part of a line from Vladimir Nabokov, "Years of secret sufferings...". Now, the whole sentence goes like this: "Years of secret sufferings had taught me superhuman self-control." The complete sentence can in no way, shape, or form be applied to this film because "superhuman self-control" is not a trait that applies to the characters that inhabit this film.

Wuthering Heights is apparently a novel by author Emily Brontë - now I've never read said novel, nor watched any of its television or film adaptions, so I walked into 2026's "Wuthering Heights" completely blind. What most attracted me to this film was the name of its director, Emerald Fennell. As a fan of all of her previous directorial efforts, I expected "Wuthering Heights" to provide more standard Fennell fare - stylish visuals, a great soundtrack, and unashamed eroticism. And this film does have all of that, though perhaps less than you might think.

First of all, yes, this movie is gorgeous, as are our two main leads, Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi. Fennel knows how to craft a lush looking film, full of intricate set design and beautiful costumes, with fitting music and songs to accompany the imagery. Margot and Jacob also have electric chemistry, and you can really feel their years-long longing for each other, especially when they finally get to engage in their desires. And said desires do lead to some fittingly sensual sequences, thanks in part due to this film's R-rating. All of that said, Fennel plays things straighter and more restrained than usual here, which actually helps make the emotions in "Wuthering Heights" hit harder.

Choosing to create more of a straightforward romance than you might expect from someone with Fennell's track record, besides a few choice moments, "Wuthering Heights" is a dramatic romance that really centers on the relationship and longing of the two main leads. Things happen in their life to drive each other away and then eventually bring them back together, and through it all you can feel their love and lust for each other practically dripping through the screen. This is an emotional film, and it does a good job in making you want to see the characters follow their heart and be with each other. And I won't say how things end up, but let's just say that if I wasn't on an SSRI I would've been weeping in the theatre.

Overall, I really liked this movie. While I do think that it may alienate audiences through a few shocking scenes, if you can look past that, you have a really effective and emotionally resonant love story; this may be too sensual or strange for some, but if you allow yourself to feel what the characters feel, I think you'll leave satisfied.

raw_payload.author: jp_91
raw_payload.content: "Wuthering Heights" is a loose adaptation of Emily Brontë's classic novel of the same name. Emerald Fennell's screenplay is intriguingly torrid and passionate, featuring erotic scenes that aren't overly explicit, and well-developed characters whose psychology, particularly their romantic relationships, envelops an intense and toxic love that permeates every scene. Fennell's direction is delightful; she knows exactly which threads to pull to inject intensity into her adaptation. Linus Sandgren's cinematography is superb, and together with the exterior and interior locations, it creates a visually intoxicating gothic atmosphere. Anthony Willis's score is exceptional, a true auditory delight. The costume design is majestic, the editing enhances the drama, and Charli XCX's songs are incredible in the select scenes where they appear. The cast is crucial, Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi have exceptional on-screen chemistry, and both deliver outstanding performances. This performances are equally tender and heartbreaking. Hong Chau delivers a unique and elevated performance, while Alison Oliver masterfully portrays a character with singular nuances, full of tenderness and voraciousness. "Wuthering Heights" is an adaptation well worth seeing, and seeing it on the big screen on February 12th, my 35th birthday, was especially memorable for me.

That said, i've always enjoyed Emerald Fennell's filmmaking style, visually, her films are striking, and she proves that again here, just like she did with Saltburn, even though i wasn't a big fan of Saltburn plot either, i could still appreciate her direction, the same goes for this film, the cinematography is beautiful, the wardrobe and production design are impressive, and the score works really well, including some Charli XCX originals that weirdly fitting for the tone.

My biggest issue lies with the story itself, specifically the central relationship between Cathy and Heathcliff, the film seems to frame them as this emotionally intense couple we're supposed to root for, even though most of their actions are destructive and morally questionable, whether intentional or not, the ending literally reinforces that idea, Heathcliff is a weirdo, by the final two acts, he feels like a completely different person, more disturbing and harder to sympathize with, for me, this turned what's meant to be a tragic love story into one of the strangest and least compelling romances i've seen.

It's also disappointing that Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi don't seem to be at their best here, Margot Robbie is clearly a strong actress, and Jacob Elordi has done some decent stuff before and he actually just delivered his best performance in the recent Guillermo Del Toro's Frankenstein, here, their performances just feel flawed and lacked,

In the end, i can appreciate almost everything about Wuthering Heights, the direction, the visuals, the music, but not its plot, its characters, or the central love story, and that's a real shame.

raw_payload.author: 1930s_Time_Machine
raw_payload.content:
There are some positives: Owen Cooper as young Heathcliff is great, there's some clever and imaginative symbolism, Margot Robbie looks gorgeous and.....no, there aren't any more. Despite the gloss, this is a hollow husk of a film without a soul which fails to engender the empathy needed to engage with it.

Admittedly capturing Emily Brontë's complex characters of this complex novel in a two hour film is virtually impossible. Even Ben Hecht and Charlie MacArthur who wrote the story for the fabulous 1939 version didn't attempt to do that, instead focussing mainly on one aspect of their personalities and the destructive power of love. Emerald Fennell certainly realises this too but somehow fails to fully explain what is driving her characters. Hecht and MacArthur had the advantage of each having twenty years of experience behind them so knew how to adapt a complicated novel into a movie - their brief, which they fulfilled brilliantly was to create two horrible, unpleasant people with whom the audience will fall in love with. So, despite them essentially re-writing the story as a dark romance you felt you knew Olivier's and Oberon's characters as much as you know your own family. The characters portrayed by Robbie and Elordi on the other hand are shallow, one-dimensional caricatures focussing just on a particular personality trait at a time giving this a disjointed, uneven feel.

Miss Brontë was heavily influenced, like many young women of her time, by Lord Byron. Her Heathcliff was clearly her fantasy Lord Byron - bad, mad and dangerous to know. In Wyler's sentimental masterpiece, although it's implicit, you just know that there's something dark and destructive about Olivier's Byronic bad boy. In this we know he's a monster because - well he says he is...we're not sure what he does that's particularly bad, he just says he's bad so we're expected to believe him. Although the 1939 version was criticised as being a sentimental love story - this tries to walk the line between being a love story and not being a love story. It tries to be about the destructive, all-consuming power of obsession, about the intense and obsessive relationship between Cathy and Heathcliff. That caustic chemistry was so obvious in the '39 picture but here you can't believe they actually know each other, let alone love each other. Without that chemistry, you can't believe in the story and also without the craziness of the ghost story, the rest of the craziness doesn't make sense either.

Although this film fails in engaging you with its shallow, unrealistic characters or indeed getting across its themes and any sense of passion whatsoever, Miss Fennell does do a really good job with her visuals. Every scene is superbly composed with so much symbolism it's coming out of its ears. I particularly liked the way she contrasted the rugged moors - out on the wily, windy moors, we'd roll and fall in green - representing the danger of freedom from constraint with the neat, safe order of The Grange which she turns into Isabella's dollhouse - a claustrophobic but pretty prison. It's just such a pity that her story didn't match the clever symbolism.

raw_payload.author: zorn907
raw_payload.content:
The sets, costumes and production design were fun, and creative, though not that interesting in the long run. It's all a mix of plastic chique, 80s fantasy TV movie, pop-avantgarde stageplay and what I imagine a Ken Russell movie to look like despite never having seen one. The whole visual language of the movie screams jewelry commercial more than anything else and Margot Robbie's costumes seemed to exist solely to fetishize her body. Without making her look actually sexy, somehow. Probably because especially in this kind of story, physical appearance isn't enough. You need some kind of exciting, spicy personalities, actors with crackling chemistry, that kind of stuff. Which this movie had none of.

The leads were way too old and came off more...in need of medical assistance rather than passionate, in love or traumatized. They either seemed to suffer from extreme late onset puberty or some sort of brain disease.

Which is in parts because of the way these characters are played.

Elordi had the charisma of a wooden stick, and almost seemed like he was doing a tasteless representation or mean imitation of someone with a learning disability. He leaned way too hard into this cartoonish dumb guy voice and I have no idea what possessed him to speak so goddamn slowly all the time. His performance ranged from dull to silly. And with silly I mean genuinely made me laugh at times.

Robbie is good, technically, I guess, crying scenes and all, but neither her demeanor nor her accent seemed anywhere near "nailing it"-territory (his accent wasn't great, either, by the way). She didn't come off immature and teenagery enough, except for whenever the plot needed her to be overly dramatic or narcissistic.

What didn't help was that he girl who played her as kid did a way better job of playing an annoying, bratty, self-absorbed and weirdly possessive person.

Not to mention the opening scene of the movie, which, not unlike Promising Young Woman, promised some macabre, sarcastic, provocative and wild ride farce of a movie. None of which was seen anywhere afterwards.

From what I understand, never having read the book, but having watched and read some reviews of the movie, its biggest issue is that it completely glosses over any sociopolitical implications stemming from either the racism or classism that seem to be very important themes as well as plot devices in the book.

The socioeconomic realities being weirdly glossed over was noticable to me even during the movie, just based on the historical context. Catherine seems more interested in the allure of marrying a rich guy than it seems to be motivated by material necessity.

And Heathcliff being some sort of ethnically ambiguous other is apparently not just some cosmetic diversity gripe bothering mostly younger US-online-individuals, but rather another major reason why Catherine and Heathcliff never consumate their love (or carnal desires) in the book. Which came as a surprise to me. That fact that they didn't I mean. Because it just...makes just so much more sense that way. Considering all those plot elements.

Also the abuse. Which would explain so much of the...few actually nasty things either of them do in the movie. While it's shown in the beginning, it seems to not matter anymore after we fast forward to them being in their mid-30s. At least that's what they look and feel like.

Catherine having what appeared to be some sort of sexual awakening/realization Heathcliff's touch makes her all high and wuthery would make sense for a 16-year-old. With a character that reads as 35 it's just weird. And without all these obstacles and mistreatments you don't have anything of actual substance standing in their way, which would create all this built-up tension, desire and longing in the first place, making them behave...not that great. :D This way they're just standing in their own way. As if they were more in love with being in pain due to unrequitted love rather than actually wanting to be together.

I actually found the plot of the movie not that uninteresting, simply because I had never read the book and didn't know anything about it. But as soon as the plot started standing still in favor of samey and half-awkward sex scenes, it got boring really fast. I saw zero chemistry, which I blame mostly Jacob Elordi and his try-hard "acting" for. He was just...weird, lanky and awkward (also the way he broke that chair earlier looked so...wimpy. :D) I heard someone say that watching these scenes made them think of people who are bad at sexting. Who type "and then I put your hand in my mouth" four times and you're just "Yes, and...?" Prior to that you could have argued that it would be hard for any actor to take their performances beyond blandness with dull dialogue like this. Even in the writing it seemed like the two leads were the most boring characters by far. But they just...weren't right for it.

I found the movie genuinely pretty funny, though, whenever it's trying to be. Be it crude pp jokes or situational comedy. Which often came from Alison Oliver. Or whenever a quick and witty retort was delivered aptly with exactly the right timing. Thinking of you, Hong Chau...and Ewan Mitchell I liked the most by far, just in general. Acting-wise. Even though he had such a small a part.

But amusing as it was, it kind of made the movie feel like it wasn't taking itself very seriously. Whereas at other times the movie was clearly expecting you to be genuinely invested in it (for some reason).

And, unique, creative or even mildly interesting all the elements that actually worked might have been, the movie never manages to weave them into anything resembling one coherent, concise piece of art. Or movie. Or anything. It's just a mish-mash of moviemaking ideas that is kind of nothing in the end. Even if mildly entertaining.

Fennell said that her approach was to focus on what she remembered from when she read the book for the first time at 14, combined with some sort of wish fulfillment as to what she would have liked to have happened. Which to her was sadomasochistic eroticism for some reason. Which...is weirdly absent from the movie considering. I mean yeah, in that one little Heathcliff/Isabella subplot, but not at all between him and Cathy. If anything they're randomly birchy and county toward each other out of the blue sometimes, but never in any kind of sexy or "sadomasochistic" way.

They're not sexy period. Even when they're dressed up to be so. Probably because they never actually act or behave in a way that is sexy. And because those real obstacles are missing.

The thing is that the repressiveness of the social norms of those times and those circumstances is exactly the thing that is supposed to create their sexual tension, which one can only assume (based on this movie) is the thing that had ol' Emerald so hot and bothered. But the movie never does anything in their stead to evoke that same kind of response. It never replaces that missing element with a real, tangible obstacle which keeps them apart. It merely visualizes Fennell's personal reaction to whatever evoked it in her when she consumed it.

Meaning it's ultimately a movie about herself, but for no-one.

It being about herself isn't an issue by itself. It's art, she's an artist (albeit not a very good one), why shouldn't it be? But it makes no attempt to actually try to instill in others what she was once given. It's like someone gave her flour and instead of making bread for everyone else, she ate it all herself and published a photo book full of pictures of her eating bread. Instead of actually sharing the food she made, she rather chooses to share impressions of what her not being hungry anymore looks like. Which won't feed anyone.

Maybe that's the real metaphor. That's the thing this movie is expressing. How much of a spoiled parasite she is (hehe). :D But yeah, this particular contortion of Wuthering Heights seems to offer no value in terms of...adding anything to the material, making it pallatable to a modern audience or providing some sort of commentary on it. Apart from what can only be described as "'elevated' horny wine mom commentary". As someone on letterboxd said. :D

No but seriously, I just don't consider this a movie for anyone. Even though it definitely seems to be an opportunity to goon over Jacob Elordi for some. Who by the way I thought was really really good in one scene. Well, more like one shot to be honest. The one where he cries over Catherine's dead body. That was some good actin'. But that was literally the only time.

It's sort of like an Eli Roth movie. Promising all kinds of nasty, disturbing, shocking, provocative goodness, but only delivering tame and bland trife.

Now having this be the plot of the Barbie movie. But with the actual abuse, darkness, trauma, racism and social politics and repression from the books...and the dick jokes from this movie...that would have indeed been quite something... :)
